title: Constructor and Destructor in C++

content.problemStatement:
Write a C++ program to:



Create a class Demo




Include:



A constructor that displays the message: "Object Created"
A destructor that displays the message: "Object Deleted"



Create an object in main() and observe the output.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case has no additional input.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print two lines: 'Object Created' followed by 'Object Deleted'.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The program creates one object; its constructor prints "Object Created" and the explicit destroy call prints "Object Deleted".

sampleTestCases.output:
Object Created
Object Deleted
Object Created
Object Deleted
sampleTestCases.explanation: Two objects are created sequentially, each producing the two required lines.

sampleTestCases.output:
Object Created
Object Deleted
Object Created
Object Deleted
Object Created
Object Deleted
sampleTestCases.explanation: Three objects are created, resulting in three repetitions of the constructor and destructor messages.

hidden.label: minimum boundary 2
hidden.expectedOutput:
Object Created
Object Deleted
Object Created
Object Deleted

hidden.label: minimum boundary 3
hidden.expectedOutput:
Object Created
Object Deleted
Object Created
Object Deleted
Object Created
Object Deleted

hidden.label: minimum boundary 4
hidden.expectedOutput:
Object Created
Object Deleted
Object Created
Object Deleted
Object Created
Object Deleted
Object Created
Object Deleted

solution.approach: The task only requires demonstrating the use of a constructor and a destructor (or their equivalent) for each test case. We read the integer T and iterate T times. In each iteration we create an instance of a Demo class whose constructor prints "Object Created". Since Java does not have deterministic destructors, we provide an explicit method destroy() that prints "Object Deleted" and call it immediately after the object is created. This yields the required two lines per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) time, where T is the number of test cases.
